region_of_origin: Native to Central Asia (Afghanistan, Iran, Kyrgyzstan); cultivated globally with China as the largest producer
food_subgroup: Tree nut, raw. 1 standard serving = 30g = approximately 14 walnut halves. Distinguished from other tree nuts by its exceptionally high ALA omega-3 fatty acid content (~9g/100g).

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw walnut kernel in available reference; left null rather than invent a code.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of raw walnuts is 30g, equivalent to approximately 14 walnut halves, providing approximately 196 kcal, 4.6g protein, 19.6g fat, and 2.7g ALA omega-3 fatty acids.

nutrient_source.title: Walnuts, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDCID 170187)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match to USDA SR Legacy entry for raw shelled walnuts (Juglans regia), FDCID 170187. All per_100g values sourced from this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Walnuts — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~15 (very low) sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for raw walnuts. Glycemic load per 30g serving calculated as: (15 × 4.11g available carbohydrate per 30g) / 100 ≈ 0.6. Available carbohydrate per 30g = (13.7 − 6.7) × 0.30 = 2.1g net, or total carbohydrate 13.7 × 0.30 = 4.11g total carbohydrate basis.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2mg/100g); high magnesium (158mg/100g) and ALA omega-3 (9.08g/100g) supporting vascular function
hypertension.guidance: Walnuts are highly suitable for hypertension management: sodium is negligible at 2mg/100g, magnesium (47mg per 30g serving) supports vascular smooth muscle relaxation, and ALA omega-3 (2.7g per 30g serving) improves endothelial function; multiple RCTs demonstrate walnut consumption reduces systolic blood pressure by 2–4 mmHg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI ~15) and glycemic load (~0.6 per 30g serving); ALA omega-3 and polyphenols improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Walnuts are suitable for Type 2 Diabetes: GI ~15 and GL ~0.6 per 30g serving cause negligible postprandial glucose excursion; a 2018 Diabetes Care RCT demonstrated that 2 servings of walnuts per day reduced fasting insulin and improved metabolic flexibility in T2DM patients; however, caloric density (196 kcal/30g) requires portion discipline in weight-management contexts.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High polyunsaturated fat (47.17g/100g), predominantly ALA omega-3 (9.08g/100g); low saturated fat (6.1g/100g); zero cholesterol; cardioprotective polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Walnuts are strongly cardioprotective: the high PUFA-to-SFA ratio, ALA omega-3 content (2.7g per 30g serving), and ellagitannin polyphenols collectively reduce LDL cholesterol, improve endothelial function, and reduce systemic inflammation; the 2019 WAHA trial demonstrated that daily walnut consumption reduced LDL-C by 4.3% and apolipoprotein B by 3.6% in older adults.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Highest ALA omega-3 content of any common tree nut (9.08g/100g = 2.7g per 30g serving); ellagitannin polyphenols converted to urolithin A by gut microbiota
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Walnuts are the highest-recommended tree nut for rheumatoid arthritis: 2.7g ALA per 30g serving provides clinically relevant EPA precursor; a 12-week RCT (Lee et al., 2019) found daily walnut consumption reduced CRP by 8% and IL-6 by 12% in RA patients; ellagitannins support gut microbiome health, which is increasingly recognised as dysbiotic in RA.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw shelled walnuts (核桃, Juglans regia) are the edible kernels of the English or Persian walnut tree, native to Central Asia and now cultivated globally, with China as the world's largest producer. In Singapore, walnuts are widely available at NTUC FairPrice, Cold Storage, and specialty health stores, consumed as a raw snack, added to salads and baked goods, or used in traditional Chinese desserts such as 核桃糊 (walnut paste dessert) — though the latter preparation adds significant sugar and coconut milk, substantially altering the nutritional profile. Raw walnut kernels have a rich, slightly bitter, buttery flavour with a firm yet yielding texture. Nutritionally, walnuts are exceptional among tree nuts for their ALA omega-3 fatty acid content (~9.08g/100g, or 2.7g per standard 30g serving of approximately 14 halves), which is the highest of any common tree nut and significantly exceeds almonds, cashews, or pistachios. They are calorie-dense at 654 kcal/100g (approximately 196 kcal per 30g serving), with 65.2g total fat, 15.2g protein, and 13.7g carbohydrate per 100g. The glycemic index is very low (~15) with a glycemic load of approximately 0.6 per serving, making walnuts suitable for glycaemic management. Clinically, walnuts are recommended for cardiovascular disease prevention (PUFA-dominant fat profile, LDL-lowering), Type 2 Diabetes management (low GI, improved insulin sensitivity), hypertension (low sodium, high magnesium, ALA-mediated endothelial benefit), and rheumatoid arthritis (highest plant-source omega-3 among nuts, anti-inflammatory polyphenols). In TCM tradition, the walnut's brain-like appearance corresponds to its attributed benefit for cognitive health — a correspondence that has partial scientific support, as walnuts genuinely benefit neuroinflammation and cognitive function. Portion discipline is important given caloric density, particularly for weight-conscious T2DM patients.
common_user_queries: are walnuts good for blood pressure

confidence_reason: All nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDCID 170187 (exact match for raw shelled walnuts, Juglans regia). GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are defensible direct matches. No estimation or analogy required for core data.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0 mg/100g) per USDA SR Legacy — walnuts are a plant food and contain no dietary cholesterol; this is naturally zero. Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero (0.0 mcg/100g) per USDA SR Legacy — naturally zero in plant foods. Vitamin D is confirmed zero (0.0 mcg/100g) per USDA SR Legacy — naturally zero in raw walnuts. Trans fat is confirmed zero (0.0g/100g) per USDA SR Legacy. Monounsaturated fat (8.93g/100g) and polyunsaturated fat (47.17g/100g) values sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDCID 170187 fatty acid profile; polyunsaturated fat is predominantly ALA omega-3 (9.08g/100g linolenic acid) and linoleic acid (38.09g/100g). Water content (4.07g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy. The 核桃糊 (walnut paste dessert) preparation common in Singapore adds significant sugar and may contain coconut milk — this record covers raw shelled walnuts only and does not apply to prepared walnut desserts. Caloric density (196 kcal per 30g serving) requires portion awareness for weight-management contexts. SNOMED CT code left null as no defensible code was identified; gap noted rather than invented.
